name: pitstop-truth
description: Machine-readable corpus of AI/API execution failure patterns. Structured receipts for retry storms, 429 misclassification, timeout cascades, and concurrency amplification — classified, verified, and cross-referenced across production systems.
thesis: When AI pipelines hit rate limits and timeouts, they often respond incorrectly — amplifying pressure instead of absorbing it. This corpus documents how, where, and why.

patterns.summary: Client retries on 429 without consulting Retry-After header. Under concurrency, all workers retry simultaneously, amplifying pressure instead of absorbing it.
patterns.fix: Consult Retry-After header before retrying. Use header value as floor for retry delay.

patterns.summary: Retry utility supports Retry-After correctly but call sites never pass the required parameter. Correct behavior is unreachable.
patterns.fix: Wire retryAfterMs at every call site that handles 429 responses.

patterns.summary: Retry-After values above threshold fall back to exponential backoff. Quota exhaustion (STOP) treated as transient rate limit (WAIT). Retries cannot succeed until quota resets.
patterns.classification: CAP vs WAIT vs STOP not distinguished
patterns.fix: Classify Retry-After > configurable threshold as STOP. Do not retry. Surface quota exhaustion explicitly.

patterns.summary: Single error message surfaces for both throughput limits (requests/min) and quota limits (weekly/session). Users cannot take correct next action.
patterns.fix: Distinguish throughput (WAIT: retry in Xs) from quota (STOP: resets at T). Surface correct signal to caller.

patterns.summary: Autonomous multi-agent system conflates quota exhaustion and transient rate limiting into a single backoff tier. Agents independently derive the WAIT/STOP distinction from first principles — confirming the taxonomy is discoverable without human guidance.
patterns.classification: STOP vs WAIT collapsed — autonomous derivation
patterns.fix: Separate backoff tiers: RATE_LIMIT_BACKOFF_FLOOR_SECS=300 for transient 429, QUOTA_BACKOFF_FLOOR_SECS=7200 for quota exhaustion. Map provider-specific error strings explicitly to hazard class before applying backoff.
patterns.note: This pattern was independently derived by autonomous agents — not reported by a human engineer. Confirms the classification problem is real enough that agents solving it themselves arrive at the same taxonomy.

patterns.summary: WAIT/CAP/STOP classification adopted as production implementation in LLM API circuit breaker within 4 hours of first contact. First external validation that the taxonomy is immediately actionable by engineers solving real agent reliability problems.
patterns.fix: 429 responses must be classified before any circuit breaker or enforcement decision runs. STOP forces CB open immediately. WAIT does not increment failure counter. CAP reduces concurrency before retry.
patterns.note: PR #1024 credits @SirBrenton explicitly. 1,631 lines of new code, 62 tests. Phase 1 covers Claude backend. Phases 2/3 extend to ChatCompletions, Response API, and Gemini. This is the first confirmed external adoption of the taxonomy as a production primitive.

patterns.summary: Structural rate limit (billing/context tier) misclassified as transient rate limit by message-pattern matching. No Retry-After header present — classification must be made from error message content. Wrong guidance surfaced: 'try again later' instead of 'reduce context or enable billing'.
patterns.classification: STOP misclassified as WAIT via message-pattern failure
patterns.fix: Add dedicated classification branch for billing/tier signals before time-pattern gate. Structural limits (billing, context tier) must be classified as STOP regardless of Retry-After presence.
patterns.note: New hazard sub-class: message-content classification failure. Previous receipts document header-value classification failure. This case has no Retry-After — the structural vs transient distinction must be made from error message content alone.

patterns.summary: Multiple client instances independently polling the same rate-limited endpoint create additive concurrency burst that exceeds limits — even when individual instance usage is low. Per-instance backoff makes it worse, not better.
patterns.fix: Designate single primary poller via file lock. Other instances read from shared cache. On 429 with no Retry-After: show last known value, reduce poll frequency. Per-instance backoff does not reduce total burst.
patterns.note: New failure shape: client-side polling amplification. Correct single-instance behavior fails predictably at N instances because burst is additive. Generalizes to any multi-instance tool sharing a rate-limited API: monitoring agents, status bars, health checkers, agent orchestrators.

patterns.summary: Request timeout triggers same exponential cooldown and auth profile rotation as explicit 429. A single transient network blip exhausts all profiles and cascades to provider fallback. Unified failure handler cannot distinguish timeout from rate limit.
patterns.classification: Timeout treated as STOP — should be WAIT with retry gate
patterns.fix: Add per-reason retry gate before cooldown. Timeout: retry same profile with jitter, only apply cooldown after retries exhausted. Explicit 429: still triggers immediate cooldown and rotation. Separate cooldown schedules per failure class.
patterns.note: Inverse of standard 429 misclassification pattern. Previous receipts document 429 treated as wrong WAIT/STOP class. This documents a non-429 failure (timeout) treated as 429-equivalent — the unified failure handler is the root cause.

patterns.summary: Rolling-window exhaustion with org-level overage disabled surfaces as identical error to throughput throttle and quota exhaustion. Dashboard shows 0-11% usage. Correct action is wait for 5-hour window reset — not 60s backoff. anthropic-ratelimit-unified-* header family is the classification signal.
patterns.classification: STOP — rolling window reset required, not throughput backoff
patterns.fix: Consult anthropic-ratelimit-unified-overage-status before applying retry logic. rejected + org_level_disabled = STOP until unified-5h-reset timestamp. Do not apply 60s backoff. Surface correct wait time to caller.
patterns.note: Third distinct 429 sub-class now documented. All three surface as identical error message: (1) throughput throttle — WAIT 60s, (2) quota exhaustion — STOP weekly reset, (3) rolling-window overage disabled — STOP 5h window reset. Smoking gun: unified-5h-utilization near 1.0 + unified-status: blocked + unified-overage-status: rejected. Full anthropic-ratelimit-unified-* header schema confirmed via post-resolution capture. Dashboard does not expose 5h window utilization.

patterns.summary: Two provider responses share the same HTTP 429 status but require different branches: transient request-frequency pressure is retryable, while quota-window exhaustion with reset time is failover-worthy STOP. When status is used without semantic body classification, failover never triggers and the same message is reprocessed repeatedly.
patterns.classification: STOP misclassified as WAIT inside failover path
patterns.fix: Classify 429s by semantic body, not status alone. Transient 'too frequent' errors take WAIT/backoff. 'Usage quota' plus 'will reset at' takes STOP/failover until reset. Do not let both share the same retry branch.
patterns.note: Strong receipt because it contrasts two semantic 429 classes under the same status code using real issue evidence. Observed consequence: same inbound message reprocessed 95+ times with zero fallback progression. Linked fix PR #58458 opened immediately after the issue.

rules: All 429s treated as retryable — CAP vs WAIT not distinguished
No max elapsed bound — retries may be unbounded

classification_model.WAIT: Transient rate limit. Retry after Retry-After duration. Pressure will subside.
classification_model.CAP: Concurrency or throughput limit. Reduce parallel requests. Do not retry immediately.
classification_model.STOP: Quota exhaustion or terminal error. Do not retry. Surface to caller. Wait for reset.
